title: The Complexity Penalty (Alpha/Lambda)
concept: The regularization parameter (alpha or lambda) controls the strength of the penalty, balancing model simplicity with training accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Recap: how does Ridge (L2) regression handle weights of less important features?

options.text: It shrinks them close to zero but keeps them all in the model
It sets them to exactly zero to delete them

exercises.explain: Correct! Ridge shrinks weights but keeps every feature. Now, let's learn about the control knob that determines how much we shrink those weights.

cards.title: The volume dial
cards.body: Think of a car radio. If the volume is at zero, you hear nothing. If it's at max, it hurts your ears and distorts the music. You need a dial to find the perfect middle volume. Regularization has a similar dial.

cards.title: The Penalty Parameter
cards.body: The strength of regularization is controlled by a hyperparameter, usually called alpha (or lambda). It multiplies the weight penalty to adjust how strictly the model is restrained.

cards.title: A tug-of-war
cards.body: Imagine a tug-of-war. On one side is training accuracy (fitting the data closely). On the other side is simplicity (keeping weights near zero). Alpha is the referee deciding which side gets more pull.

cards.title: Tuning the knob
cards.body: If alpha is set to 0, the penalty has no effect (standard regression). If alpha is set to 1000, the penalty dominates, crushing all weights close to zero and making the model too simple.

cards.title: Is higher always better?
cards.body: Since regularization prevents overfitting, you might think a higher alpha is always better. But if alpha is too high, the model undergoes underfitting — it becomes too simple to capture even basic patterns.

cards.title: Finding the sweet spot
cards.body: How different alpha values affect the model's fit:

right.text:
Just right.
Captures the trend, ignores noise.

exercises.prompt: What happens if you set the regularization parameter alpha (or lambda) to zero?

options.text: The model becomes standard regression with no regularization
All weights are set to exactly zero

exercises.explain: When alpha is zero, the penalty term is multiplied by zero, removing it completely. You get standard, unregularized regression.

exercises.prompt: True or false: setting a very high alpha value always improves the model's accuracy on new test data.
exercises.explain: False. Too much regularization (high alpha) will make the model underfit, crushing the weights so much that it cannot learn the real patterns.

exercises.sentence: The parameter that controls the strength of regularization is commonly called alpha or ___.
exercises.explain: Depending on the software library or textbook, this regularization knob is called either alpha or lambda.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes a model that is underfitting because the regularization parameter alpha is set too high?

options.text: A model predicting almost the exact same flat price of $200,000 for every house, regardless of size
A model predicting house prices with 98% accuracy on training data but only 50% on test data
A model that fits the general trend of the market with small, stable weights

exercises.explain: When alpha is too high, it shrinks weights close to zero, creating a flat, over-simplified model that underfits.

exercises.prompt: You run a Ridge regression model with alpha = 0.1 and get high accuracy on training data but poor accuracy on test data (overfitting). You rebuild it with alpha = 1000 and get terrible accuracy on both (underfitting). What should you do next?

options.text: Try an intermediate alpha value, like 1.0 or 10.0, to find the sweet spot
Keep alpha at 1000 and add more noisy features
Give up on regularization and go back to alpha = 0
Multiply all weights manually by 10 to adjust them

exercises.explain: Exactly! Hyperparameter tuning is about finding the sweet spot between overfitting (alpha too low) and underfitting (alpha too high). Try something in the middle! 🎉
